name: Korybantes
description: The Korybantes were ecstatic and armed male dancers with connections to the worship of Cybele, the Phrygian mother goddess. They were often associated with the Curetes, Dactyls, and Telchines, other groups of mythical beings known for their wild dances and rituals. The Korybantes' dances were characterized by frenzied movements, loud music from tambourines, cymbals, pipes, and shouting, all intended to induce a state of religious ecstasy. These rituals were believed to protect the infant Zeus from his father Cronus, who sought to devour him. By clashing their weapons and creating a deafening din, they masked the baby Zeus's cries, allowing him to be hidden away safely on Crete. They are sometimes depicted as armed and crested, performing their war-like dances in honor of Cybele. Different accounts give varying origins for the Korybantes, some claiming they were born from the earth, others attributing their parentage to Apollo and Thalia, or even to Cybele herself. Their primary role was to serve as guardians and protectors, and their association with wild, ecstatic rituals made them a significant part of the ancient Greek religious landscape, representing the untamed and powerful aspects of nature and divinity.
subtitle: Ecstatic dancers and protectors of Zeus.
appearance: The Korybantes are typically depicted as young men, often armed with shields, swords, and spears. They wear crested helmets and ornate armor, reflecting their role as warriors and protectors. Their faces are often depicted as wild and expressive, reflecting the ecstatic nature of their dances. They are sometimes shown with animalistic features, such as goat legs or horns, emphasizing their connection to the untamed forces of nature.
habitat: The Korybantes were associated with mountainous regions, forests, and caves, particularly in Phrygia and Crete, where they performed their rituals. They were also believed to inhabit sacred groves and temples dedicated to Cybele.
behavior: The Korybantes are characterized by their frenzied and ecstatic behavior. They engage in wild dances, accompanied by loud music and shouting, to induce a state of religious ecstasy. They are fiercely protective of those under their care, particularly infants and those dedicated to Cybele. Their behavior is often described as chaotic and unpredictable, reflecting the untamed nature of the goddess they serve.

keyMyths.title: Protection of Zeus
keyMyths.description: The Korybantes famously protected the infant Zeus from his father Cronus by drowning out his cries with their loud music and clashing weapons, allowing him to be hidden away safely on Crete. This act ensured Zeus's survival and eventual rise to power.

keyMyths.title: Rituals of Cybele
keyMyths.description: The Korybantes were integral to the worship of Cybele, performing frenzied dances and rituals in her honor. These rituals involved loud music, ecstatic movements, and the clash of weapons, all intended to induce a state of religious fervor and connect with the divine.

sources.author: Apollodorus
